clinicalStem: A 24-week gestation male infant is born by emergency caesarean section for placental abruption. Birth weight is 620 g. He is intubated at delivery and admitted to the NICU. Surfactant is given and he is placed on high-frequency oscillatory ventilation. On day 7, cranial ultrasound reveals bilateral grade IV intraventricular haemorrhage with periventricular leukomalacia. Parents ask the neonatologist about the expected long-term neurological prognosis. Which of the following best describes his most likely neurodevelopmental outcome?
options: Normal neurodevelopment with mild school-age learning difficulties only
High risk of severe cerebral palsy, intellectual disability, and visual impairment
Isolated language delay with full motor recovery expected
Normal outcome if he is discharged home without seizures
Hearing impairment as the predominant long-term deficit
explanation: Bilateral grade IV IVH (haemorrhagic infarction of periventricular white matter) combined with periventricular leukomalacia in a 24-week infant carries a very high risk of severe neurodevelopmental impairment including spastic cerebral palsy, intellectual disability, and cortical visual impairment. These lesions destroy periventricular white matter tracts critical for motor and cognitive development. The prognosis is substantially worse than isolated grade I–II IVH.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Grade IV bilateral IVH with PVL is far more serious than causing mild school difficulties; this degree of white matter destruction typically produces major motor and cognitive deficits.
Correct — bilateral grade IV IVH plus PVL predicts severe cerebral palsy, intellectual disability, and often cortical visual impairment.
Motor pathways run through the periventricular white matter; isolated language delay is inconsistent with bilateral grade IV lesions and PVL which cause spastic quadriplegia.
Absence of seizures at discharge does not negate the structural brain damage already visible on ultrasound; seizures may emerge later and outcome is determined by the lesion, not discharge status.

Hearing impairment is a risk in prematurity (especially with aminoglycoside use) but is not the predominant deficit predicted by bilateral grade IV IVH and PVL; motor and cognitive impairment dominate.
clinicalPearl: Bilateral grade IV IVH (periventricular haemorrhagic infarction) with PVL carries the worst neurodevelopmental prognosis among all grades of IVH in preterm infants.
keyClue: Bilateral grade IV IVH + periventricular leukomalacia on day 7 cranial ultrasound in a 24-week infant.
commonExamTrap: Assuming absence of seizures predicts a good outcome — structural injury drives prognosis, not seizure status at discharge.
missedConcept: Grading of IVH and its correlation with long-term neurodevelopmental prognosis; grade IV = periventricular haemorrhagic infarction, the most severe form.

clinicalStem: A 28-week gestation female infant, now 36 weeks corrected gestational age, is being assessed prior to NICU discharge. Her birth weight was 980 g. She required supplemental oxygen for the first 6 weeks of life, receiving up to 60% FiO2. She is now breathing room air. Parents ask when her eyes should be checked for retinopathy of prematurity (ROP). At what corrected age and by what criterion should the FIRST ophthalmology screening be performed?
options: At 6 months corrected age, since she is now on room air
At 31 weeks postmenstrual age or 4 weeks after birth, whichever is later
Only if there is a visible red reflex abnormality on routine examination
At term equivalent age (40 weeks PMA) as a one-time screen
Immediately at discharge regardless of postmenstrual age
explanation: ROP screening should begin at 31 weeks postmenstrual age (PMA) or at 4 weeks of chronological age, whichever is later. This guideline applies to infants born at ≤30 weeks gestation or with birth weight ≤1500 g, or selected higher-gestational-age infants with an unstable clinical course. Delaying beyond this window risks missing treatable stage 3+ disease. This infant at 28 weeks gestation should have first screening at 31 weeks PMA (3 weeks after birth), or at 4 weeks chronological age if 31 weeks PMA has not yet been reached.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Waiting until 6 months corrected age is far too late; active ROP can progress to retinal detachment well before that, and the window for treatment would be missed.
Correct — first ROP screening is at 31 weeks PMA or 4 weeks chronological age, whichever is later, per standard neonatal guidelines.
ROP has no external signs detectable on routine exam until very advanced stages; screening requires indirect ophthalmoscopy by a trained ophthalmologist regardless of red reflex.
A one-time screen at 40 weeks PMA would miss the critical treatment window for threshold or pre-threshold ROP which often presents at 32–36 weeks PMA.
Screening at discharge alone is insufficient — follow-up ophthalmology visits are required based on postmenstrual age, not solely on discharge timing.
clinicalPearl: ROP screening begins at 31 weeks PMA or 4 weeks of life (whichever is later) for all infants ≤30 weeks gestation or ≤1500 g birth weight.
keyClue: 28-week infant with prolonged oxygen exposure — high-risk for ROP requiring structured ophthalmology screening.
commonExamTrap: Assuming that being on room air at discharge removes the ROP risk — oxygen exposure during the vulnerable retinal vascularisation phase has already occurred.
missedConcept: ROP screening criteria are based on gestational age and chronological age thresholds, not on current oxygen requirement.

clinicalStem: A 2-day-old male term neonate presents with poor feeding, temperature instability, and a petechial rash spreading across the trunk. He was born via normal vaginal delivery after prolonged rupture of membranes (22 hours). On examination: HR 190 bpm, RR 72/min, capillary refill time 4 seconds, and he appears poorly perfused. Investigations reveal WBC 2.1 × 10⁹/L, platelets 28 × 10⁹/L, PT 38 seconds, APTT 95 seconds, fibrinogen 0.6 g/L, and blood culture is pending. Which of the following is the most likely cause of his haematological abnormalities?
options: Vitamin K deficiency bleeding
Neonatal alloimmune thrombocytopenia
Disseminated intravascular coagulation secondary to neonatal sepsis
Congenital factor VIII deficiency
Physiological prolongation of clotting times in the newborn
explanation: This neonate has clinical sepsis (prolonged ROM, poor perfusion, fever, tachycardia) with evidence of DIC: thrombocytopenia, markedly prolonged PT and APTT, and critically low fibrinogen. DIC is a life-threatening complication of neonatal sepsis caused by systemic inflammatory activation of the coagulation cascade, consuming platelets and clotting factors. The petechial rash in this context reflects thrombocytopenia and microthrombi rather than isolated platelet disorder.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Vitamin K deficiency (haemorrhagic disease of the newborn) causes prolonged PT but platelet count is normal and fibrinogen is preserved; it does not explain the thrombocytopenia or systemic sepsis picture.
Neonatal alloimmune thrombocytopenia causes isolated severe thrombocytopenia in an otherwise well infant — coagulation times and fibrinogen are normal, and there are no signs of systemic illness.
Correct — DIC secondary to neonatal sepsis explains the combination of thrombocytopenia, prolonged PT/APTT, and hypofibrinogenaemia in a systemically unwell neonate.
Factor VIII deficiency (haemophilia A) prolongs APTT only, platelet count and PT are normal, and it does not explain the systemic sepsis picture.

While term neonates have slightly prolonged PT/APTT compared to adults, the values here are grossly abnormal with concurrent thrombocytopenia and hypofibrinogenaemia — this cannot be attributed to physiological variation.
clinicalPearl: DIC in neonatal sepsis is characterised by thrombocytopenia, prolonged PT and APTT, and low fibrinogen — treat the underlying sepsis and provide supportive haemostatic therapy.
keyClue: Prolonged ROM → sepsis + petechiae + thrombocytopenia + prolonged PT/APTT + low fibrinogen = DIC.
commonExamTrap: Attributing the coagulopathy to vitamin K deficiency — VK deficiency does not cause thrombocytopenia or hypofibrinogenaemia.
missedConcept: DIC is a recognised and dangerous complication of neonatal sepsis; recognising the full coagulation profile (not just one abnormal value) is essential.
redFlagReason: This neonate is in septic shock with DIC — a rapidly fatal condition requiring immediate IV broad-spectrum antibiotics, fluid resuscitation, and haemostatic support. Delay in recognition and treatment is life-threatening.

clinicalStem: A 10-day-old female neonate is treated for Group B Streptococcus meningitis confirmed by CSF culture. She initially seizes on day 2 of illness but seizures are controlled with phenobarbitone. She is discharged at 4 weeks after completing a 21-day course of IV penicillin G. At her 6-month follow-up, she is noted to have no eye tracking, poor head control for age, and increased tone in all four limbs. An MRI brain reveals multicystic encephalomalacia. Which long-term complication is she MOST at risk of developing in addition to cerebral palsy?
options: Insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus
Sensorineural hearing loss
Coarctation of the aorta
Hypothyroidism
Nephrotic syndrome
explanation: Sensorineural hearing loss (SNHL) is one of the most common and important sequelae of neonatal bacterial meningitis, occurring in up to 30% of GBS meningitis survivors. It results from direct cochlear and auditory nerve inflammation and ischaemia. All survivors of neonatal meningitis require formal audiological assessment (ABR/DPOAE). In a child already showing signs of cerebral injury, coexistent SNHL significantly worsens developmental outcomes and speech acquisition.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Type 1 diabetes is not a recognised complication of neonatal bacterial meningitis; it is autoimmune in origin and unrelated to CNS infection.
Correct — sensorineural hearing loss occurs in up to 30% of neonatal meningitis survivors due to cochlear and auditory nerve damage from the infectious and inflammatory process.
Coarctation of the aorta is a congenital cardiac anomaly unrelated to meningitis sequelae.
Hypothyroidism is not a complication of neonatal meningitis; it results from thyroid dysgenesis or dyshormonogenesis.
Nephrotic syndrome is a renal disorder unrelated to the neurological sequelae of bacterial meningitis.
clinicalPearl: All neonatal meningitis survivors must have formal audiological testing (ABR) before discharge and at follow-up — SNHL occurs in up to 30% and is treatable with early hearing aids or cochlear implants.
keyClue: Confirmed GBS meningitis in a neonate — SNHL is a major and actionable complication requiring structured screening.
commonExamTrap: Focusing only on neurological/motor deficits and missing the need to screen for sensorineural hearing loss after neonatal meningitis.
missedConcept: Neonatal meningitis causes multiple co-morbidities including SNHL, which must be actively screened for even when neurological injury is apparent.

clinicalStem: A 38-week gestation male infant is delivered by emergency caesarean section for a prolonged cord prolapse. He is floppy and apnoeic at birth; Apgar scores are 1 at 1 minute and 4 at 10 minutes. He requires 8 minutes of positive pressure ventilation before establishing spontaneous respirations. At 45 minutes of life he is irritable, hypertonic, and has abnormal eye movements. Arterial cord blood gas: pH 6.92, base deficit −18 mmol/L. Amplitude-integrated EEG (aEEG) shows a moderately abnormal background. He is admitted to the NICU. What is the MOST appropriate next step in management to improve his long-term neurological outcome?
options: Administer IV phenobarbitone empirically to prevent seizures
Initiate whole-body therapeutic hypothermia (33–34°C) within 6 hours of birth
Perform an urgent MRI brain to guide management decisions
Start prophylactic broad-spectrum antibiotics pending sepsis screen
Administer IV dextrose to treat hypoglycaemia-associated encephalopathy
explanation: This infant meets criteria for therapeutic hypothermia (TH): ≥36 weeks gestation, evidence of perinatal asphyxia (cord pH <7.0, base deficit ≥16), and clinical signs of moderate-to-severe HIE with abnormal neurological examination and abnormal aEEG. TH at 33–34°C for 72 hours, initiated within 6 hours of birth, is the only intervention proven to reduce death and neurodisability in HIE. The window of opportunity is the latent phase before secondary energy failure commences.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Empirical phenobarbitone is not indicated without confirmed electrographic seizures; the priority is initiating cooling which itself reduces seizure burden in HIE.
Correct — therapeutic hypothermia (33–34°C) within 6 hours is the evidence-based intervention for moderate-to-severe HIE in infants ≥36 weeks, and must not be delayed.
MRI brain is an important prognostic tool but is typically performed at 4–7 days after cooling; it should not delay the decision to commence TH, which is time-critical.
Antibiotics may be appropriate if sepsis is suspected, but they do not address the primary pathology (HIE) and should not be the lead management step here.
There is no evidence of hypoglycaemia in the stem, and dextrose administration does not treat HIE; glucose management is a supportive adjunct, not the primary intervention.
clinicalPearl: Therapeutic hypothermia (33–34°C for 72 h) must be started within 6 hours of birth in infants ≥36 weeks with moderate-to-severe HIE — the 6-hour window is the critical treatment deadline.
keyClue: pH 6.92 + base deficit −18 + abnormal neurology + abnormal aEEG in a ≥36-week infant = meets cooling criteria.
commonExamTrap: Choosing MRI as the first step — MRI is prognostic, not therapeutic, and must not delay time-critical cooling.
missedConcept: TH is the only neuroprotective therapy with proven benefit in HIE; delay beyond 6 hours eliminates its benefit.
redFlagReason: HIE with a 6-hour treatment window — failure to initiate therapeutic hypothermia promptly significantly worsens neurodevelopmental outcome and mortality.

topic: Neonatal seizures — follow-up and antiepileptic discontinuation
clinicalStem: A term male neonate was diagnosed with moderate HIE and treated with therapeutic hypothermia. During the NICU stay he had electrographic seizures confirmed on continuous EEG, treated with phenobarbitone. At discharge on day 14, he has no further seizures, is feeding well, and MRI brain shows restricted diffusion in the bilateral thalami but no cortical injury. EEG on day 12 shows mild background slowing. His mother asks whether he will need phenobarbitone long term. Which of the following BEST guides the decision on antiepileptic duration post-discharge?
options: He should continue phenobarbitone indefinitely since thalamic injury predicts epilepsy
Phenobarbitone can be stopped before discharge if seizure-free for 72 hours
The decision should be guided by EEG normalisation and neurology follow-up, typically weaning at 3 months if seizure-free
All neonates with HIE-related seizures require antiepileptics until age 2 years
Phenobarbitone should be replaced immediately with levetiracetam for safer long-term use
explanation: There is no consensus mandating indefinite antiepileptic therapy after neonatal seizures unless the infant has recurrent clinical seizures, a highly epileptogenic EEG pattern, or a structural epilepsy syndrome. Current practice favours early discontinuation (often before discharge or within the first 3 months) in infants who are seizure-free, guided by EEG normalisation and clinical neurodevelopmental assessment at follow-up. Thalamic injury on MRI raises the risk of later epilepsy but does not mandate permanent antiepileptic therapy without electroencephalographic or clinical evidence of ongoing seizures.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Thalamic injury increases epilepsy risk but indefinite antiepileptic therapy is not automatically indicated; ongoing seizures or EEG evidence of epileptiform activity are required to justify long-term treatment.

While some centres discontinue phenobarbitone before discharge in seizure-free term infants with resolved EEG, 72 hours seizure-free is not a universal standalone criterion — EEG and clinical assessment should inform the decision.

Correct — antiepileptic duration is guided by seizure recurrence, EEG background normalisation, and neurodevelopmental follow-up; weaning at ~3 months in a seizure-free infant with improving EEG is standard in many centres.
No guideline mandates antiepileptics until age 2 for all HIE-related neonatal seizures; this is an overtreatment approach that exposes infants to unnecessary drug side effects.
There is no strong evidence that levetiracetam is safer than phenobarbitone long-term in neonates, and switching without clear indication is not evidence-based practice.
clinicalPearl: Neonatal seizure antiepileptic therapy is often temporary — review at follow-up with EEG before committing to long-term treatment, as many neonatal seizures do not predict childhood epilepsy.
keyClue: Seizure-free at discharge with mild EEG slowing — clinical and EEG follow-up, not automatic indefinite therapy, guides discontinuation.
commonExamTrap: Assuming any neonatal seizure or thalamic MRI injury mandates lifelong antiepileptic therapy.
missedConcept: Neonatal seizures are often provoked and transient; antiepileptic discontinuation is appropriate in many cases and should be guided by EEG and clinical follow-up rather than a fixed duration.

clinicalStem: A 40-week female infant is born following a traumatic forceps delivery with a tight nuchal cord. She requires brief mask ventilation at birth (2 minutes) and recovers to Apgar 7 at 10 minutes. At 1 hour of life she is jittery and hyper-alert with mild hypotonia, brisk Moro and tendon reflexes, and a mild sucking difficulty. She has no seizures. EEG shows normal background voltage and continuity. MRI brain at day 4 is normal. Which of the following BEST describes her prognosis?
options: High likelihood of severe neurodisability; therapeutic hypothermia should be started immediately
Moderate risk of cerebral palsy; she requires prolonged NICU admission for monitoring
Favourable prognosis; the majority of infants with mild HIE have normal neurodevelopmental outcomes
Normal short-term outcome but high risk of childhood epilepsy
She will need cochlear implants due to high risk of sensorineural hearing loss from birth asphyxia
explanation: This infant has features of mild HIE (Sarnat stage I): hyperalertness, jitteriness, brisk reflexes, mild hypotonia, no seizures, normal EEG background, and normal MRI. Mild HIE carries an excellent prognosis — the large majority (>90%) of these infants have normal neurodevelopmental outcomes at 18 months and beyond. Therapeutic hypothermia is generally not indicated for mild HIE. Normal EEG and MRI at day 4 are strongly reassuring prognostic markers.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Severe neurodisability is associated with severe HIE (Sarnat stage III); this infant has mild HIE with normal EEG and MRI — cooling is not indicated and the prognosis is favourable.
Prolonged NICU admission and high risk of cerebral palsy reflect moderate-to-severe HIE; this infant's mild stage and normal investigations do not warrant this level of concern.
Correct — mild HIE (Sarnat I) with normal EEG and normal day 4 MRI predicts a normal neurodevelopmental outcome in the great majority of cases.
Childhood epilepsy is primarily a risk with moderate-to-severe HIE associated with cortical injury on MRI; normal MRI in mild HIE does not predict high epilepsy risk.
Sensorineural hearing loss from birth asphyxia is associated with severe perinatal compromise, not mild HIE with a rapid clinical recovery and no aminoglycoside exposure.
clinicalPearl: Mild HIE (Sarnat stage I) with normal EEG and normal MRI predicts a favourable neurodevelopmental outcome — overcautious counselling leads to unnecessary parental anxiety.
keyClue: No seizures, normal EEG background, normal MRI, and hyper-alert (not encephalopathic) infant = mild HIE with good prognosis.
commonExamTrap: Automatically instituting therapeutic hypothermia based on the delivery history without staging HIE severity — cooling is for moderate-to-severe HIE only.
missedConcept: HIE staging (Sarnat I/II/III) directly determines both management (cooling) and prognosis; all three stages must be differentiated.

topic: Biliary atresia — prognosis after Kasai portoenterostomy
clinicalStem: A 6-week-old female infant presents with persistent jaundice since birth, acholic (pale) stools, and dark urine. Conjugated bilirubin is 112 µmol/L (total bilirubin 148 µmol/L). Liver ultrasound shows an absent or atretic gallbladder. Intraoperative cholangiogram confirms biliary atresia. She undergoes a Kasai portoenterostomy at 7 weeks of age. At 3-month follow-up, her bilirubin has normalised. Her parents ask about her long-term outlook. Which of the following BEST describes the prognosis for a child with biliary atresia who has a successful Kasai procedure (bilirubin normalisation)?
options: She is cured and requires no further hepatology follow-up
She is likely to develop biliary cirrhosis and require liver transplantation by adolescence regardless of bilirubin normalisation
Approximately 25–40% of children with biliary normalisation after Kasai survive with their native liver into adulthood, but ongoing surveillance for portal hypertension is essential
She will need immediate listing for liver transplantation as Kasai is only a bridge procedure valid for 6 months
Prognosis is entirely determined by the age at Kasai — normalised bilirubin has no prognostic significance
explanation: After Kasai portoenterostomy, bilirubin normalisation is the single most important predictor of native liver survival. Approximately 25–40% of patients who achieve bilirubin normalisation will survive with their native liver into adulthood. However, progressive hepatic fibrosis can still occur even in those with clearance of jaundice, and lifelong hepatology surveillance is required to monitor for portal hypertension, oesophageal varices, and hepatopulmonary syndrome. The majority of biliary atresia patients eventually require liver transplantation, but successful Kasai clearly delays and in some cases avoids it.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Bilirubin normalisation is a positive prognostic sign but does not mean cure — ongoing fibrosis, portal hypertension, and cholangitis remain risks that require lifelong hepatology surveillance.

While most biliary atresia patients eventually need transplantation, a significant proportion (25–40%) with successful Kasai achieve long-term native liver survival into adulthood; universal adolescent transplant is not the expected course.
Correct — ~25–40% of children with bilirubin clearance after Kasai survive with their native liver into adulthood, but all require lifelong follow-up for portal hypertension and disease progression.
Immediate transplant listing after a successful Kasai is incorrect; the goal of Kasai is to achieve bile drainage and delay/prevent transplantation, not as a 6-month bridge.

Age at Kasai is important (outcomes are better before 60–75 days), but bilirubin normalisation post-Kasai is the strongest independent predictor of native liver survival and has significant prognostic value.
clinicalPearl: Bilirubin normalisation after Kasai portoenterostomy is the strongest predictor of native liver survival — about 25–40% survive into adulthood without transplant, but all need lifelong hepatology monitoring.
keyClue: Biliary atresia with bilirubin normalisation post-Kasai — does not mean cure; long-term surveillance for cirrhosis and portal hypertension is mandatory.
commonExamTrap: Assuming normalised bilirubin = cure and no further follow-up needed — progressive fibrosis and portal hypertension can occur even with jaundice clearance.
missedConcept: Kasai portoenterostomy buys time and in some cases avoids transplant, but biliary atresia is a progressive disease requiring lifelong hepatology care.

clinicalStem: A 4-month-old male infant with confirmed biliary atresia who has not yet undergone Kasai portoenterostomy presents with a 2-day history of generalised tonic-clonic seizures. He has been exclusively breastfed. Physical examination reveals hepatosplenomegaly, jaundice, and bruising over the limbs. Investigations: total bilirubin 210 µmol/L, conjugated 168 µmol/L, INR 4.2, platelets 180 × 10⁹/L. Which of the following is the MOST likely immediate cause of his seizures and bruising?
options: Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) deficiency causing seizures
Thrombocytopenia from hypersplenism
Fat-soluble vitamin K deficiency causing haemorrhagic disease
Neonatal meningitis complicating chronic liver disease
Hyperammonaemia from acute liver failure
explanation: Prolonged cholestasis impairs bile acid delivery to the gut, critically reducing the absorption of fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K). Vitamin K deficiency causes a severe coagulopathy (markedly elevated INR) which can manifest as haemorrhage, including intracranial bleeding causing seizures. This is a recognised and preventable complication of untreated biliary atresia. The markedly elevated INR (4.2) with normal platelet count and clinical bleeding in a cholestatic infant strongly points to vitamin K–dependent coagulation factor deficiency.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Pyridoxine (B6) deficiency is a very rare cause of neonatal seizures unrelated to cholestasis; it does not explain the elevated INR or bruising.
Thrombocytopenia from hypersplenism causes isolated low platelet count; here platelets are normal (180 × 10⁹/L) and the bleeding diathesis is explained by the markedly elevated INR.
Correct — vitamin K deficiency from fat malabsorption in prolonged cholestasis causes severe coagulopathy (INR 4.2) leading to haemorrhage and seizures (likely intracranial bleed).

Meningitis would present with fever, meningism, and CSF abnormalities; this presentation is fully explained by coagulopathy from vitamin K deficiency and does not require a separate infectious diagnosis.

Hyperammonaemia from acute liver failure causes encephalopathy and may cause seizures, but in a chronic cholestatic infant INR 4.2 with normal bilirubin trend points primarily to coagulation factor deficiency rather than acute liver failure.
clinicalPearl: In cholestatic infants, fat-soluble vitamin K malabsorption causes life-threatening coagulopathy — monitor INR and supplement fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) from diagnosis.
keyClue: Prolonged cholestasis + INR 4.2 + normal platelets + bruising + seizures = vitamin K deficiency haemorrhagic disease.
commonExamTrap: Attributing bleeding to thrombocytopenia (hypersplenism) when the platelet count is normal but the INR is markedly elevated.
missedConcept: All four fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) must be supplemented in neonatal cholestasis to prevent deficiency complications; vitamin K deficiency is acutely dangerous.
redFlagReason: Vitamin K–deficient intracranial haemorrhage causing seizures is a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate IV vitamin K, urgent imaging, and neurosurgical assessment.

clinicalStem: A 3-day-old female infant is noted to have petechiae, hepatosplenomegaly, thrombocytopenia (platelets 42 × 10⁹/L), and jaundice. Head circumference is on the 2nd centile. Cranial ultrasound shows periventricular calcifications. CMV DNA is detected in urine by PCR on day 2 of life (confirming congenital infection). Newborn hearing screening shows bilateral refer on both ears. Parents are counselled about antiviral therapy with valganciclovir. Which of the following BEST describes the evidence-based rationale for treating this infant with valganciclovir?
options: Valganciclovir eradicates CMV from the infant and prevents recurrence of infection
Treatment prevents periventricular calcifications from enlarging and causing hydrocephalus
Six months of valganciclovir improves hearing outcomes and neurodevelopmental scores compared with no treatment in symptomatic congenital CMV
Valganciclovir is indicated only for infants with CMV retinitis and not for systemic disease
Antiviral therapy is contraindicated in neonates due to neutropenia risk; treatment should be deferred until age 3 months
explanation: The CASG 109 and related trials demonstrated that 6 months of oral valganciclovir (versus 6 weeks) in infants with symptomatic congenital CMV (CNS involvement) resulted in significantly better hearing outcomes at 12 and 24 months and improved neurodevelopmental scores. Treatment does not eradicate CMV but suppresses viral replication during the critical neurodevelopmental window, reducing progressive cochlear and CNS damage. This infant has symptomatic congenital CMV with CNS involvement (periventricular calcifications, microcephaly) and hearing loss — a clear indication for treatment.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Valganciclovir suppresses viral replication but does not eradicate CMV; the virus persists latently and can reactivate later in life.
Valganciclovir reduces ongoing viral-mediated neurological injury but does not reverse established calcifications; the goal is to limit progressive damage, not to resolve existing lesions.
Correct — 6 months of valganciclovir in symptomatic congenital CMV with CNS involvement improves hearing and neurodevelopmental outcomes based on randomised controlled trial evidence.

Valganciclovir is indicated for all infants with symptomatic congenital CMV, particularly those with CNS involvement; retinitis is a criterion in immunocompromised patients but not the sole indication in neonates.

Neutropenia is a known side effect requiring monitoring (regular FBC), but antiviral therapy is not contraindicated in neonates — it is actively recommended in symptomatic disease; the risk-benefit clearly favours treatment.
clinicalPearl: Symptomatic congenital CMV with CNS involvement should be treated with 6 months of oral valganciclovir — RCT evidence shows improved hearing and neurodevelopmental outcomes.
keyClue: Periventricular calcifications + microcephaly + bilateral hearing loss + CMV PCR positive on day 2 = symptomatic congenital CMV requiring valganciclovir.
commonExamTrap: Confusing congenital CMV treatment criteria with CMV retinitis in immunocompromised adults — neonatal indication is broader (any CNS involvement).
missedConcept: Duration matters — 6 months of valganciclovir outperforms 6 weeks for long-term hearing and developmental outcomes in symptomatic congenital CMV.

topic: Complications of prematurity — chronic lung disease
clinicalStem: A 26-week gestation male infant, now at 36 weeks corrected gestational age, remains on 32% supplemental oxygen via nasal cannula. He was ventilated for 6 weeks, received two courses of surfactant, and has required supplemental oxygen continuously since birth. Chest X-ray shows hyperinflation with areas of atelectasis and cystic changes. His weight gain is poor despite adequate caloric intake of 150 kcal/kg/day. Which of the following complications is MOST directly responsible for his ongoing poor weight gain?
options: Congenital hypothyroidism causing metabolic failure to thrive
Increased caloric expenditure from the elevated work of breathing in bronchopulmonary dysplasia
Short bowel syndrome from necrotising enterocolitis
Cow's milk protein allergy causing malabsorption
Iron-deficiency anaemia suppressing appetite
explanation: Bronchopulmonary dysplasia (BPD) is defined by the requirement for supplemental oxygen beyond 36 weeks PMA in a preterm infant. Infants with BPD have markedly increased metabolic demands due to the elevated work of breathing, combined with feeding difficulties (oral aversion, gastro-oesophageal reflux). These factors cause poor weight gain despite seemingly adequate caloric provision. Energy requirements in moderate-to-severe BPD can exceed 150–160 kcal/kg/day, necessitating caloric fortification or gastric tube feeding.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Congenital hypothyroidism is routinely screened at birth and would present with other features (constipation, coarse facies, prolonged jaundice); this is not the most direct cause of poor weight gain in this clinical scenario.
Correct — the chronically elevated work of breathing in BPD dramatically increases caloric expenditure, making weight gain difficult even at seemingly adequate intake of 150 kcal/kg/day.
There is no mention of NEC or intestinal resection in the history; while NEC is a complication of prematurity, it is not the cause of poor growth in this presentation.
Cow's milk protein allergy presents with GI symptoms (bloody stools, vomiting) and is not related to the respiratory history of BPD.
Iron-deficiency anaemia can reduce energy levels and appetite but is not the primary driver of poor weight gain in a ventilated preterm infant with established BPD and respiratory compromise.
clinicalPearl: Infants with BPD have increased caloric requirements (often >150–160 kcal/kg/day) due to elevated work of breathing — caloric density of feeds must be increased beyond standard to achieve adequate growth.
keyClue: Persistent oxygen need at 36 weeks PMA + hyperinflation with cysts on CXR = BPD; elevated work of breathing drives caloric expenditure.
commonExamTrap: Assuming 150 kcal/kg/day is always sufficient — BPD substantially increases metabolic rate and caloric need beyond standard preterm targets.
missedConcept: BPD increases caloric expenditure through chronic elevated work of breathing; nutritional targets must be adjusted upward accordingly.

topic: Approach to neonatal cholestasis — early diagnosis of biliary atresia
subtopic: Stool colour and timing of Kasai
clinicalStem: A 5-week-old female infant is referred by her general practitioner for persistent jaundice. She was born at term, birth weight 3.4 kg, and was discharged at 48 hours. Her mother reports pale, chalky-white stools for the past 2 weeks and dark urine. She is breastfed and gaining weight adequately. Examination: deeply jaundiced sclera, mild hepatomegaly, no splenomegaly. Conjugated bilirubin is 98 µmol/L; total bilirubin 126 µmol/L. GGT is markedly elevated. Ultrasound shows a small, irregular gallbladder. What is the MOST important reason for URGENT referral to a paediatric hepatology or surgical centre at this age?
options: To arrange immediate liver biopsy to confirm the diagnosis before any intervention
To perform the Kasai portoenterostomy before 60–75 days of age when outcomes are significantly better
To initiate ursodeoxycholic acid therapy as the definitive curative treatment
To screen the mother for primary sclerosing cholangitis as the likely inherited cause
To arrange an immediate liver transplantation as Kasai is not effective beyond 4 weeks of age
explanation: Biliary atresia is a surgical emergency in neonatal cholestasis. The Kasai portoenterostomy is the primary surgical intervention, and its success is strongly time-dependent — outcomes are significantly better when performed before 60–75 days of age (approximately 8–10 weeks). After this window, progressive obliteration of residual biliary remnants and advancing hepatic fibrosis dramatically reduce the rate of bile drainage achievable by Kasai. Urgent referral at 5 weeks is appropriate to allow diagnostic workup (including intraoperative cholangiogram) and timely surgery within the optimal window.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Liver biopsy may be part of the diagnostic workup but is not the reason for urgent referral — the time-critical priority is surgical Kasai before 60–75 days, not tissue diagnosis.
Correct — Kasai portoenterostomy outcomes are substantially better before 60–75 days; this 5-week-old must be urgently referred to ensure surgery occurs within the optimal window.
Ursodeoxycholic acid is a supportive agent for cholestasis but is not curative for biliary atresia; it does not replace surgical drainage.
Biliary atresia is not an inherited condition linked to maternal PSC; it is a progressive obliterative cholangiopathy of likely multifactorial aetiology.
Kasai can be effective well beyond 4 weeks of age — the upper limit of the optimal window is 60–75 days; liver transplantation is considered when Kasai fails or is performed too late.
clinicalPearl: In neonatal cholestasis, biliary atresia is a surgical emergency — every day counts. The Kasai portoenterostomy must be performed before 60–75 days of age for optimal bile drainage and native liver survival.
keyClue: Acholic stools + dark urine + conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia + irregular small gallbladder on USS at 5 weeks = biliary atresia until proven otherwise — urgent surgical referral.
commonExamTrap: Delaying referral to complete outpatient investigations — the time-dependent window for Kasai success means any delay risks worse surgical outcomes.
missedConcept: The 60–75-day Kasai window is the most important prognostic factor in biliary atresia that clinicians can modify — urgency of referral is the key management principle.
redFlagReason: Biliary atresia is a progressive obliterative cholangiopathy — failure to perform Kasai within the 60–75-day window leads to cirrhosis, liver failure, and death without transplantation.
